title: Iterative divider off ALU critical path
motivation:
The current ALU implements RV32IM DIV/REM with combinational / and % operators
in the same execute-stage result mux as the common integer and multiply ops.
CoreMark is dominated by adds, branches, loads, stores, and multiplies; rare
division hardware can still cap FPGA Fmax and consume LUTs even when it is not
on the dynamic hot path.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current ALU implements RV32IM DIV/REM with combinational / and % operators

CoreMark is dominated by adds, branches, loads, stores, and multiplies; rare

on the dynamic hot path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace only DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU with a small iterative divider/rem unit and
stall the single-issue pipeline while that unit is busy. Keep ADD/SUB/logic,
shifts, compares, address generation, branches, jumps, and all MUL/MULH
variants single-cycle. This trades latency on rare divide instructions for a
shorter EX-stage combinational path and lower LUT pressure, which should raise
median Tang Nano 20K Fmax without materially changing CoreMark cycles.

title: Decouple RV32M from EX critical path
motivation:
The current EX stage contains the full RV32IM ALU, including three 64-bit multiply products and combinational divide/remainder logic. Even when CoreMark is executing ordinary RV32I ALU, branch, and memory instructions, that wide M-extension datapath remains part of the synthesized execute-stage cone and likely limits Fmax near the Tang Nano 20K LUT budget.

A structural split that keeps common RV32I operations single-cycle while moving RV32M work into a dedicated busy/ready unit should raise Fmax and reduce routing pressure. CoreMark uses multiply but rarely needs divide in the timed loop, so a modest multiply latency penalty should be outweighed by a shorter critical path.

title: Static IF backward branch predictor
motivation: The current five-stage core resolves all branches and jumps in EX and redirects on every taken control transfer. CoreMark spends much of its timed region in tight loops, so backward loop branches repeatedly pay a wrong-path bubble even though their target is statically knowable from the fetched instruction. A small static predictor can recover those bubbles while preserving the single-issue RVFI contract and avoiding a large Fmax hit.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Add IF-stage predecode for conditional branches and JAL. Predict JAL as taken, predict conditional branches as taken only when the B-immediate is negative, and otherwise predict fall-through. Carry the predicted next PC through ID/EX, then change EX redirect generation to redirect only when the resolved next PC differs from the prediction. This should remove most loop-back branch bubbles in CoreMark with only modest mux/add/sign-extension logic in the fetch path and no stateful predictor tables.

title: Share RV32M multiplier datapath
motivation:
After moving DIV/REM out of the combinational ALU, the remaining RV32M
multiplier logic is a likely Fmax and routing limiter. The current ALU
computes signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, and signed*unsigned 64-bit
products in parallel even though each instruction consumes only one result.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the three parallel product nets with a single sign-controlled
extended multiply selected from the ALU_MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU operation.
MUL can still use the low 32 bits, while the high-half variants select the
correct signedness through operand extension. This should reduce LUT/DSP
duplication and the ALU result-mux/routing cone without changing pipeline
latency, CoreMark instruction count, RVFI behavior, or ALTOPS semantics.

title: Decouple fetch with skid queue
motivation:
The current frontend treats every low io_imemReady cycle as an immediate
decode bubble, and backend stalls hold the PC so the core cannot fetch ahead
while memory, load-use, or iterative divide latency is already stopping
retirement. In the tournament cosim mode both instruction and data buses see
random stalls, so overlapping independent frontend availability with backend
stalls can improve CoreMark cycles without relying on the failed IF-stage
instruction predecode path.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current frontend treats every low io_imemReady cycle as an immediate
decode bubble, and backend stalls hold the PC so the core cannot fetch ahead

retirement. In the tournament cosim mode both instruction and data buses see
random stalls, so overlapping independent frontend availability with backend

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Insert a small two-entry instruction skid/prefetch queue between the fetch PC
and ID. The fetch side advances and enqueues whenever io_imemReady is high and
the queue is not full; the decode side consumes only when the existing backend
hazard logic allows ID to advance. Redirects flush the queue and restart fetch
at the resolved target, preserving precise RVFI retirement and the NRET=1 port
contract.

This structural decoupling should hide a fraction of random iStall cycles
behind dmem stalls, load-use bubbles, and divide busy cycles, while adding only
a shallow FIFO and PC muxes instead of putting branch predecode on the
imem_data-to-next_pc critical path.

title: PC-indexed BTB predictor
motivation:
The previous static predictor recovered some loop-control bubbles but put
instruction predecode and immediate generation on the fetch next-PC path,
which is risky for Tang Nano 20K Fmax. CoreMark still has repeated taken
loop branches and some direct/indirect jumps, so a predictor can help if the
lookup path is driven only by the registered fetch PC rather than by
io_imemData.

hypothesis.motivation:

instruction predecode and immediate generation on the fetch next-PC path,
which is risky for Tang Nano 20K Fmax. CoreMark still has repeated taken
loop branches and some direct/indirect jumps, so a predictor can help if the
lookup path is driven only by the registered fetch PC rather than by
io_imemData.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small direct-mapped PC-indexed BTB with a 2-bit taken counter and target
per entry. IF predicts taken only on a tag hit with a taken counter and uses
the stored target; otherwise it fetches pc+4. EX resolves the actual next PC,
updates the table for branches and jumps, and redirects only when the resolved
next PC differs from the carried prediction.

This should recover most steady-state CoreMark loop-back bubbles after warmup
without the failed imem_data predecode critical path. The cost is a small
PC-indexed LUT/register table and a compare/mux in IF, so the expected gain is
modest but more Fmax-safe than static fetch predecode.

title: Late load-store data bypass
motivation:
The current load-use detector stalls whenever a load in EX has an rd
matching either raw source field of the following IF/ID instruction. That is
necessary for ALU operands, branch compares, and store address bases, but it
is over-conservative for a STORE whose rs2 data is not consumed until MEM.
CoreMark's timed kernels perform many load/store moves and immediate-form
operations, so avoidable one-cycle bubbles directly reduce iterations per
cycle without helping correctness.

hypothesis.motivation:

matching either raw source field of the following IF/ID instruction. That is
necessary for ALU operands, branch compares, and store address bases, but it

CoreMark's timed kernels perform many load/store moves and immediate-form

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Refine the load-use hazard to distinguish operands needed in EX from store
data that can be supplied one stage later. For a following STORE, keep the
stall only when the load feeds rs1, the address base; allow an rs2-only
dependency to advance and resolve it with a MEM-stage bypass from the current
MEM/WB result into the store-data path. The bypass must also update the
retiring store's RVFI rs2_rdata and mem_wdata fields so formal/cosim observe
the architectural source value.

Default unknown or illegal encodings to conservative source use in the
hazard predecode, preserving trap and RVFI behavior. This should recover IPC
from load-to-store and false rs2-immediate hazards while adding only a small
MEM data mux, avoiding the fetch critical-path costs seen in prior predictor
attempts and the EX multiplier regressions from prior micro-opts.

title: One-entry RAM store buffer
motivation:
The current in-order pipeline freezes behind every STORE whenever io_dmemReady
is low, even though most stores do not produce a value needed by the following
instruction. In the tournament cosim mode dmem sees random backpressure, so
CoreMark pays avoidable full-pipeline bubbles on ordinary RAM stores.

Prior structural attempts put new logic on the fetch path or tried to decouple
the whole M-extension unit. A narrower backend store buffer should attack a
common stall source while leaving fetch, branch resolution, RVFI width, and the
working iterative divider structure unchanged.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry write buffer for aligned, non-MMIO RAM stores. When a store
reaches MEM and the dmem bus is not ready, capture its address, byte mask, and
replicated write data into the buffer, retire the store once architecturally
enqueued, and let younger non-memory instructions continue. Drain the buffer
to dmem as soon as io_dmemReady returns.

Preserve ordering by stalling any younger load/store while the buffer is full
unless the pending store has drained, and keep UART/timing-marker MMIO stores
unbuffered so CoreMark brackets and output timing remain tied to real bus
acceptance. This should hide a fraction of random dmem store stalls with only
a small MEM-stage register/mux addition and minimal Fmax risk.

title: One-entry hot redirect predictor
motivation:
The previous predictor attempts show that CoreMark has exploitable taken loop
control flow, but larger or instruction-predecode-based predictors hurt the
Tang Nano 20K fetch critical path enough to erase the cycle savings. The most
promising remaining predictor angle is to keep the frontend prediction logic
deliberately tiny and only target the hottest repeated redirect.

hypothesis.motivation:
The previous predictor attempts show that CoreMark has exploitable taken loop
control flow, but larger or instruction-predecode-based predictors hurt the
Tang Nano 20K fetch critical path enough to erase the cycle savings. The most
promising remaining predictor angle is to keep the frontend prediction logic
deliberately tiny and only target the hottest repeated redirect.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry tagged redirect cache in IF. On an exact fetch-PC tag hit,
predict the cached target as the next PC; otherwise fetch pc+4. EX carries the
predicted-next metadata, resolves the actual next PC for every retiring
instruction, redirects only on a prediction mismatch, updates the entry on
taken branches and jumps, and invalidates it when the same PC resolves not
taken or non-control.

This recovers steady-state bubbles for one dominant CoreMark loop backedge or
repeated jump while avoiding the failed imem_data predecode path and avoiding
a multi-entry BTB/BHT table. The hardware cost should be one valid bit, one
PC tag compare, one target register, and a next-PC mux, so the Fmax risk is
much lower than the prior predictor designs.

title: Iterative high-half multiply
motivation:
The successful divider change showed that removing rare RV32M operations from
the common EX-stage combinational cone can raise FPGA Fmax without materially
hurting CoreMark cycles. The current ALU still computes three 64-bit multiply
products in parallel for MUL, MULH, MULHU, and MULHSU, even though CoreMark's
timed hot path is expected to use ordinary low-half MUL far more often than
high-half multiply variants.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep ALU_MUL as a single-cycle low-half multiply, but move MULH, MULHU, and

execute-busy stall pattern. This should let synthesis reduce the common ALU
multiplier to only the low 32 result bits for MUL, while removing the signed
and signed/unsigned high-product cones from the normal ADD/branch/load/store
execute path.

Unlike the prior shared 33x33 multiplier attempt, this does not add a wider
signedness mux to every multiply operation. The latency penalty is paid only
for high-half M-extension instructions, which should be rare or absent in the
CoreMark timed region, while RV32M correctness remains covered by dedicated
unit tests and cosim.

title: Split high-half multiplier
motivation:
The best bench result came from removing cold divide/remainder hardware from
the common execute-stage ALU path, while later attempts that added fetch
queues, predictors, or store buffering lost more Fmax than they recovered in
cycles. The remaining ALU still builds signed*signed, unsigned*unsigned, and
signed*unsigned 64-bit multiplier cones even though CoreMark's hot path is
expected to use ordinary low-half MUL far more often than MULH/MULHU/MULHSU.

A narrower structural split should preserve CoreMark's common single-cycle
multiply behavior while removing the high-half signedness/product logic from
the timing-critical EX result mux.

motivation:
Prior predictor attempts on this core regressed because they put too much
target-generation or tag-lookup logic on the shallow fetch critical path.
CoreMark still contains repeated call/return control flow in addition to
loops, and returns are the one dynamic-control case where a tiny predictor
can provide a target without a BTB target array or branch-immediate adder.

motivation:
The strongest prior result came from shortening a real timing cone without

title: Pipelined full multiplier unit
motivation:
The best measured improvement came from removing cold divide/remainder logic from
the common execute-stage ALU path. The remaining wide arithmetic limiter is the
single-cycle RV32M multiplier cone: the ALU still builds multiple 64-bit products
in the same result path used by ordinary adds, branches, address generation, and
stores. Prior high-half-only attempts regressed because low-half MUL stayed in
that common cone, so the critical path was not structurally removed.

too much logic to the shallow fetch next-PC path. CoreMark still has repeated
counted-loop backedges, but the predictor must be narrower than those failed
designs to have any chance of improving fitness.

title: Resetless LUTRAM register file
motivation:
Prior structural attempts that added frontend predictors, fetch queues, store buffering, or multi-cycle multiplier control lost more Fmax than they recovered in CoreMark cycles. The remaining low-risk structural target is the integer register file: it is currently a resettable 32x32 flop bank even though only x0 has architectural zero behavior, and the full synchronous reset adds muxing and routing to every stored bit.

Retargeting this storage without changing pipeline timing should improve FPGA placement/routing pressure while preserving the existing single-issue CPI, hazard behavior, forwarding, and RVFI contract.

title: Valid-only pipeline payload resets
motivation:
The latest winning bench change improved fitness by pruning dead pipeline

title: MEM-to-EX load bypass
motivation:
The current pipeline pays a one-cycle load-use interlock whenever a load in
EX feeds either raw source field of the following instruction. CoreMark's hot
loops are load-heavy, and the successful recent changes show that this core
is already Fmax-competitive enough that recovering real CPI bubbles may be a
better structural bet than adding more prediction or buffering state.

Prior structural attempts that added fetch queues, predictors, store buffers,
or multi-cycle multiply control regressed, so this proposal keeps the pipeline
shape and NRET=1 RVFI contract intact while only adding a local load-result
bypass around the existing MEM/EX boundary.

address from EX/MEM. Then remove the blanket load-use stall for consumers
that can safely wait under the existing dmem stall mechanism.

it rather than allowing a stale redirect. This should eliminate many common
load-to-ALU, load-to-address, and load-to-store-data bubbles while preserving
precise RVFI source values, trap behavior, and memory ordering.

title: Registered lookahead backedge predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts found exploitable CoreMark loop control flow but lost
more Fmax than they recovered because branch predecode, BTB lookup, tag
compares, or return recognition landed directly on the shallow fetch next-PC
path. The remaining promising predictor angle is to keep the PC update path
driven only by registered state while still learning one hot loop backedge.

Add a single-entry conditional-backedge predictor with a one-cycle lookahead
lookup. When EX resolves a taken backward conditional branch, store its branch
PC, the preceding sequential PC, and the target. While fetching normally, IF
compares the current PC against the stored preceding PC and registers whether
the next sequential instruction is the learned branch; on the following cycle,
the PC mux uses only that registered predict-valid bit and registered target.

This should recover steady-state bubbles for a dominant CoreMark loop branch
without putting a BTB table, current-PC tag compare, instruction predecode, or
branch-immediate adder on the same-cycle fetch next-PC path. EX still carries
predicted-next metadata, redirects precisely on mismatch, and disables the
entry on loop exit or aliasing, preserving RVFI order, trap behavior, and the
NRET=1 top-level contract.

title: Share EX add and compare cones
motivation:
The bench history shows the best improvements came from removing timing and
routing pressure without adding new prediction, memory-ordering, or M-unit
control. The current EX stage still spells out several equivalent 32-bit PC
adders and branch comparisons in separate expressions that feed redirect,
pc_next, JAL link writeback, and RVFI pc_wdata. Even if synthesis shares some
of this logic, making the sharing explicit is a low-risk way to reduce the
branch/redirect cone that CoreMark exercises heavily.

title: Registered writeback result path
motivation:

title: Redirect target replay predictor
motivation:
Prior predictor attempts found real CoreMark loop-control opportunity, but every design that changed the normal fetch next-PC path with predecode, PC compares, target muxes, or prediction metadata lost more Fmax than it recovered in CPI. The remaining predictor angle should avoid making every sequential fetch pay for loop prediction.

A tiny target-instruction replay buffer can target the same repeated taken backedges without predicting the PC ahead of time. It only acts on an actual EX redirect and tries to fill the redirect bubble with a previously observed target instruction, so the common pc+4 fetch path stays essentially unchanged.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry redirect-target replay predictor inside IF. When a taken branch or jump redirects to a target and the target instruction is fetched normally, record the target PC and instruction word. On a later EX redirect to the same target, emit the cached target instruction and target PC into ID during the redirect cycle instead of forcing a NOP, while still updating the real PC to the redirect target.

This should recover one bubble for hot loop backedges after warmup without a BTB lookup, current-PC tag compare, imem_data branch predecode, or predicted-next-PC metadata on normal fetch cycles. Mismatches are not speculative branch predictions: the buffer is only used after EX has already resolved a redirect, and it should be disabled whenever the pipeline is held by dmem/ex_busy or the target entry is not valid. Program memory is immutable for the benchmark, so replaying the cached instruction for the same target PC preserves RVFI instruction/PC behavior.

title: Split RVFI order counter
motivation:
Recent successful bench changes improved fitness by trimming timing and routing
pressure without adding frontend prediction, memory bypasses, or new pipeline
state. The top-level RVFI order counter still increments a full 64-bit value on
every retirement, and that counter is synthesized into the FPGA build even
though CoreMark only needs monotonic retirement numbering for checking.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the single 64-bit rvfi_order_q + 1 incrementer in rtl/core.sv with a
split low/high counter: increment a 32-bit low half every valid retirement and
increment the high half only when the low half wraps. Drive the RVFI order port
from the concatenated halves, preserving reset-to-zero and strict +1 monotonic
ordering across all retirements.

This keeps the NRET=1 port contract, retirement boundary, IPC, trap behavior,
and all memory behavior unchanged. If the current FPGA timing graph includes
the 64-bit RVFI carry chain or its routing fanout, shortening it to two smaller
counters should modestly raise median Fmax with essentially no CoreMark cycle
cost. If the order counter is not timing-relevant, the change should be nearly
neutral.

title: Split writeback bypass from regfile reads
motivation:
The strongest positive bench changes have removed fanout and muxing from existing pipeline paths rather than adding predictors, buffers, or extra memory machinery. The register file still has write-first read bypass muxes on both asynchronous read ports, so every ID-stage source read carries same-cycle WB address compares and write-data muxing even though most dynamic reads do not hit the current WB instruction.

Prior resetless-regfile work showed the reset tree was not the interesting part of this block; the remaining structural target is the read/write bypass coupling itself. Moving the rare same-cycle WB dependency into the existing EX forwarding network should shorten the imem-data-to-ID/EX register-file read path without changing CoreMark CPI.

title: Tiny ifetch replay predictor
motivation:
Prior branch predictors for this core consistently regressed because they put
target lookup, instruction predecode, prediction metadata, or repair muxing on
the shallow fetch/redirect path. The apples-to-apples CoreMark mode also has
roughly 22% random instruction-bus stalls, and the current frontend turns every
low io_imemReady cycle into a NOP even when the same hot-loop PC was fetched
correctly on an earlier iteration.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny direct-mapped instruction replay predictor trained only by accepted
instruction fetches. On an io_imemReady=1 fetch, record {PC tag, instruction}
in an 8-entry table. On a later io_imemReady=0 cycle, if the current PC hits
the table, supply the cached instruction and treat fetch as effectively ready;
otherwise keep the existing stall/NOP behavior.

This is not a branch target predictor and does not carry prediction metadata
into EX. It attacks repeated hot-loop iStalls while avoiding the failed IF
branch-predecode/BTB/RAS/backedge-repair paths. Because the instruction memory
is immutable in FPGA/cosim and formal ties io_imemReady high, a replay hit is
architecturally the same instruction word previously observed for that PC.

sensitive to routing and control fanout. Earlier micro-opt wins came from
pruning dead control and unnecessary reset muxing, and the replay table only
needs deterministic validity after reset, not deterministic payload contents.

title: Wider resetless ifetch replay cache
motivation:
The latest winning change showed that the safest remaining cycle gain is not
branch prediction, but replaying immutable hot-loop instructions when the
random instruction bus stalls. The current replay table is only 8 entries and
direct-mapped, so CoreMark loop bodies can still conflict on pc[4:2] and fall
back to bubbles even after the instruction was previously fetched.

Prior structural attempts that added queues, store buffers, bypass paths, or
predictor metadata regressed because they lengthened common control paths.
A larger direct-mapped replay cache keeps the same no-repair frontend shape
while improving the hit rate of the one mechanism that has already produced a
large fitness win.

title: Mask false load-use stalls
motivation:
The current load-use interlock compares a load destination against both raw
source fields of the following instruction, even when the following opcode
does not consume rs2 or does not consume any register source. That creates
occasional one-cycle bubbles after loads for OP-IMM, LOAD, JALR, LUI, AUIPC,
JAL, FENCE, and other non-rs2 consumers whenever immediate bits alias the
load rd. Prior lessons show added bypass datapaths often cost too much, so
the safest remaining IPC micro-opt is to remove only false stalls without
adding a new data path.

boundary is therefore a better structural bet than adding more speculation.

title: Folded-index ifetch replay predictor
motivation:
The only predictor that has produced a large bench improvement is the accepted-fetch instruction replay table, because it hides random hot-loop iStalls without branch-target lookup, predecode, or EX repair metadata. Follow-up replay changes that widened the table, repacked lines, warmed during backend stalls, or filtered redirect fills all regressed, so the next predictor step should preserve the same simple lookup semantics and avoid adding new storage depth or control paths.

The current 8-entry table is indexed directly by pc[4:2], so instructions separated by 32-byte strides always evict each other. CoreMark loop bodies and call-heavy code can easily contain hot PCs with that spacing, causing replay misses even after each instruction has been fetched successfully before.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the 8-entry accepted-fetch replay predictor exactly the same size and keep its fill-on-io_imemReady, replay-only-when-io_imemReady-is-low semantics, but replace the direct pc[4:2] index with a cheap folded hash such as pc[4:2] ^ pc[7:5]. Keep the existing full replay tag comparison so replay hits remain exact for the immutable instruction memory contract.

This targets direct-mapped conflict misses without the larger array, half-line muxing, backend warm cursor, redirect filter, branch predictor metadata, or next-PC target muxes that hurt prior predictor attempts. The hardware cost is only a few XOR gates feeding the existing 3-bit replay index, so any recovered hot-loop iStall hits should have a better chance of improving CoreMark fitness than another branch predictor or wider replay cache.

title: Derive funct3 controls locally
motivation:
The best prior micro-opt wins came from removing cold datapath logic and pruning
pipeline control state, while recent replay and predictor follow-ups regressed
when they added fetch-path muxing or metadata. The current core still carries
several control bits that are direct copies of instruction funct3 fields even
though the full instruction word already travels through the pipeline for RVFI.
Those redundant bits do not improve CPI, but they add decode outputs, stage
flops, and routing pressure in a design that appears fanout-limited on FPGA.

hypothesis.motivation:

Those redundant bits do not improve CPI, but they add decode outputs, stage

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Remove redundant funct3-derived control payload from the pipeline. Derive the
branch comparison op in EX from the ID/EX instruction bits, derive load/store
width and load signedness in MEM from the EX/MEM instruction bits, and derive
the MEM/WB load-result select from mem_read rather than carrying mem_to_reg
through ID/EX and EX/MEM. The decoder should still validate every opcode and
funct field exactly as today, with is_illegal defaulting high, but it no longer
needs to export these simple mirror controls.

This keeps the five-stage timing, accepted-fetch replay table, NRET=1 RVFI
contract, trap semantics, memory alignment behavior, and CoreMark instruction
count unchanged. If the current implementation is still limited by decode and
pipeline-control routing, trimming these bits should modestly improve median
Fmax without risking the cycle-count regressions seen from predictor, replay,
multiplier, and bypass experiments.

title: Operand-aware load interlock
motivation:
The current hazard unit stalls after every load when the load destination
matches either raw register field of the following instruction. That is
conservative for RVFI, but it also creates false bubbles for immediates,
jumps, upper-immediate ops, fences, and traps whose bit fields alias the
load rd without being execution operands. CoreMark has many load-adjacent
pointer and immediate operations, so recovering even a small fraction of
these bubbles can improve iterations per cycle.

A prior false-load-use attempt broke because unused raw rs1/rs2 fields still
need correct RVFI register data. The structural opportunity remains if the
core separates execution operand readiness from RVFI source reporting instead
of treating every raw field as a data dependency.

need correct RVFI register data. The structural opportunity remains if the

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add explicit source-use metadata for the instruction entering ID/EX and use
it only for the load-use interlock. Branches, stores, JALR, and R-type ops
still stall when they consume a load result, but I-type immediates, LUI,
AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, EBREAK, and illegal instructions no longer stall solely
because an unused raw field aliases the older load rd.

Preserve RVFI correctness by keeping the raw rs1/rs2 addresses and adding a
small RVFI-source repair path for unused aliased fields. Existing MEM/WB
forwarding covers older writes; the one-cycle load-ahead case should take the
MEM-stage sign/zero-extended load value into the EX/MEM RVFI rs value field
without feeding the ALU operand mux. This keeps the actual execute operands
and redirect path conservative while allowing false interlocks to disappear.

This is a structural split between operand consumption and retirement
reporting, not a predictor or fetch replay change. It keeps NRET=1, raw RVFI
source reporting, trap discipline, and memory ordering intact while targeting
CPI instead of adding more fetch-path timing risk.

Gate the load-use interlock with rs1_used/rs2_used so unused raw fields no longer create false load bubbles.
Separate ALU operand forwarding from RVFI source-value capture and repair unused aliased RVFI source values from load-data forwarding.
Expose the MEM-stage extended load result as a sideband RVFI repair source without changing dmem request behavior or writeback data.
Wire the source-use metadata and MEM load repair sideband between ID, hazard, MEM, and EX while preserving the NRET=1 top-level port shape.

title: Victim-slot ifetch replay predictor
motivation:
Branch-target predictors, RAS variants, countdown loop predictors, and
replay-table capacity/hash changes have mostly regressed because extra PC
muxing, EX repair metadata, or wider fetch routing cost more Fmax than they
recovered in CoreMark cycles. The one predictor that improved this core is
the accepted-fetch instruction replay table, which hides random hot-loop
instruction stalls without predicting control flow.

The remaining plausible replay weakness is direct-mapped thrashing between a
small number of hot PCs that share pc[4:2]. A single victim slot targets that
specific failure mode without widening the primary table or changing the
normal pc+4 fetch path.

hypothesis.motivation:
Branch-target predictors, RAS variants, countdown loop predictors, and
replay-table capacity/hash changes have mostly regressed because extra PC
muxing, EX repair metadata, or wider fetch routing cost more Fmax than they
recovered in CoreMark cycles. The one predictor that improved this core is

The remaining plausible replay weakness is direct-mapped thrashing between a
small number of hot PCs that share pc[4:2]. A single victim slot targets that
specific failure mode without widening the primary table or changing the
normal pc+4 fetch path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the current 8-entry accepted-fetch replay predictor as the primary
lookup, but add one fully-tagged victim replay slot. When an accepted fetch
replaces a valid primary entry with a different tag, move the evicted tag and
instruction into the victim slot. On io_imemReady=0, first check the indexed
primary entry; if it misses, check the victim slot and replay its instruction
on a tag hit. Do not predict branches or jumps, do not add predicted-next-PC
metadata, and do not alter redirect handling.

This should recover iStall bubbles from two-way hot conflicts that the
direct-mapped replay table cannot hold, while adding only one extra tag
compare and one replay-data mux on the stalled-fetch path. It is narrower
than the failed 16-entry, folded-index, line-packed, or warm-prefetch replay
variants and preserves the immutable-instruction correctness argument.

changes.description: Add a one-entry victim replay slot beside the existing 8-entry accepted-fetch table; fill it with evicted primary entries and consult it only on io_imemReady-low primary misses.
